Jiang Qin, styledGongyi, was fromShouchun[壽春],Jiujiang[九江].
WhenSun Cewas reliant onYuan Shu,[Jiang] Qinfollowed and tendered his service, and when[Sun] Ceferrieda cross to the east, he was conferred to beMajor of a Separate Unit, being given troops. He and[Sun] Cetraveled and pacified three commanderies,1and he also followed along in stabilizingYuzhang[豫章]. He was transferred to serve asCounty CommandantofGeyang[葛陽尉], then served asChiefof three counties in succession. He suppressed bandits and was madeCommandant of the West Sector. The bandits ofZhi[冶] inKuaiji[會稽],Lü HeandQin Lang, among others, caused chaos and[Jiang] Qinled troops to suppress and attack them. He thus captured[Lü] Heand[Qin] Lang. Five counties were pacified. He was transferred to becomeHousehold General who Suppresses the [Shan]yue.Jingju[涇拘] andZhaoyang[昭陽] were made taxpaying cities for him. WhenHe Qiwas suppressing bandits that were inYou[黝],[Jiang] Qindirected ten thousand troops and joined forces with[He] Qi. The bandits ofYou[黝] werepacified.•••
The “three commanderies” presumably refer toWu commandery,Danyang commandery, andKuaiji commandery.↩
He followed in the expedition againstHefei[合肥].WeigeneralZhang Liaoattacked[Sun] Quannorth of a certainford.2[Jiang] Qinfought hard and earned merits. He was promoted toGeneral who Sweeps Away Enemiesand becameMarshalofRuxu[濡須督]. Later, he was summoned to return to the capital. He was conferred asRight Army Protectorof theFordand audited legalcases.•••
Here, “a certain ford” refers to theXiaoyaojin fordinHuainan. This is also the same ford the title “Right Army Protectorof theFord” references.↩
[Sun] Quanonce entered his household. His mother had coarse curtains and pale blue blankets while the wife and concubines had cloth skirts.[Sun] Quanadmired him for maintaining frugality despite being in a noble position. He immediately issued an edict that bade the imperial warehouse to make brocade blankets and change the curtains. The clothes of his wife and concubines were all of beautiful brocade.
Previously, when[Jiang] Qingarrisoned inXuancheng[宣城], he once suppressed bandits that were inYuzhang[豫章]. ThePrefectofWuhu[蕪湖令],Xu Sheng, arrested a barracks official of[Jiang] Qinand petitioned to behead him.[Sun] Quandid not consent because[Jiang] Qinwas far away.3[Xu] Shengthus thought he received ill will from[Jiang] Qin. WhenExcellency Caomarched toRuxu[濡須],[Jiang] QinandLü Mengwielded military tallies.4[Xu] Shengwas often terrified that[Jiang] Qinwould take advantage of the situation to harm him, but[Jiang] Qinalways appraised him of being good.[Xu] Shengwas thus convinced that he was virtuous. Debaters regarded this asadmirable.A•••
It is unknown whyXu Shengarrested the barracks official, but regardless, it seemsSun QuanwantedJiang Qinto make a judgment once he returned from suppressing enemies rather than execute the barracks official immediately.↩
This is to say thatJiang QinandLü Mengled armies againstCao Cao.↩
TheBiographies of the Region South of the Riverstates:[Sun] Quantold[Jiang] Qin, “In the past,[Xu] Shengreported you, sir. In the present, you recommended[Xu] Sheng. Is your intent to emulateQi Xi?”5[Jiang] Qinreplied, “I know to make recommendations out of impartiality and to not embrace personal grievances.[Xu] Shengis loyal, and he is diligent and strong. His talent in daring strategies can be put to use, and he would be a good marshal for ten thousand soldiers. With important matters unresolved in the present, I should help the state seek the talented. How could I dare embrace my personal grievances in order to conceal the worthy?”[Sun] Quanadmiredthis.•••
Qi Xihad recommended those who disliked him to office.↩
When[Sun] Quanwas suppressingGuan Yu,[Jiang] Qinsupervised the navy to enter theMian[沔]. During the return, he fell ill on the road and died.[Sun] Quanwore white mourning clothes and grieved. Two hundred households of people inWuhu[蕪湖] and a field of two hundred hectares were conferred to the wife and children of[Jiang] Qin.
His son,[Jiang] Yi, was enfeoffed asMarquisofXuancheng[宣城侯].6He led troops to resistLiu Beiand gained merits. He returned toNan commandery[南郡], but clashed withWeiand died in battle.[Jiang] Yihad no sons. His younger brother,[Jiang] Xiu, led troops, who later was deprived of the enterprise7due to having donecrimes.•••
Zhuge Jin was enfeoffed asMarquisofXuanchengshortly afterGuan Yuwas defeated according to his biography. This contradicts this passage, and it is not known who is correct.↩
“Enterprise” [業] refers to the hereditary right of the progeny of generals to lead the troops of their father.↩

Jiang Qin (Gongyi)

Jiang Qin, styled Gongyi, was from Shouchun in Jiujiang Commandery. He was an attendant under Sun Ce when he attacked Yuan Shu. When Sun Ce sailed back east, Jiang Qin was made Major with a Separate Command and given a military command. He accompanied Sun Ce in his expeditions around, and pacified the three commanderies [that were to be the base of Wu’s power]. After that he followed Sun Ce to conquer Yuzhang. Later, he was transferred to be Commandant of Geyang and then to govern three prefectures. During his tenure, he vanquished the Shanyue bandits and eventually was made Chief Commandant of the West. When the bandits Lü He and Qin Lang from Ye in Huiqi stirred up five prefecture, including Kuaiji and Dongye prefectures, in rebellion, Jiang Qin led troops to fight them, and captured the two rebels. As those five prefectures were pacified, he was relocated and promoted as the Imperial Guard General Who Defeats the Yue. He was also given Jingqu and Zhaoyang as his fiefs.
When He Qi went to fight the bandits at Yi, Jiang Qin joined him with ten thousand men and together they defeated the rebels. Jiang Qin was also a member of Sun Quan’s expedition to Hefei. When the Wei general Zhang Liao assailed Sun Quan at North Jin, Jiang Qin fought bravely and had many merits, and so Sun Quan made him General Who Terrifies Criminals, and Controller of Ruxu. Later on, he was recalled to the capital, made Commissioner of the Army of the Right, and given additional duties at the legal court.
Once, Sun Quan walked into the inner halls of Jiang Qin’s house, and found that Jiang’s mother only had a light bed-curtain and cotton blankets, and his wives and concubines wore only simple cloth dresses. Sun Quan, greatly impressed by Jiang Qin’s virtue of living a frugal life despite his high rank, ordered his own household to make silken blankets for Jiang’s mother, and to exchange her curtains for better ones. His wives and concubines were given silken and embroidered clothes.
In an earlier time, Jiang Qin was on guard at Xuancheng, and was out fighting the bandits at Yuzhang. Xu Sheng, the prefect of Wuhu at that time, arrested an official of Jiang Qin’s, and requested [Sun Quan] for permission to behead him. As Jiang Qin was away, Sun Quan denied the request, but since then Xu Sheng estranged himself from Jiang Qin. Some time after that, Cao Cao lead an army out of Ruxu, and Jiang Qin and Lü Meng were made in charge of military discipline. Xu Sheng was constantly worried that Jiang Qin would find some reason to cause him harm. However, instead of that, Jiang Qin frequently gave high praises of Xu Sheng. At that, Xu Sheng became convinced of Jiang Qin’s virtues, and the men who discussed these things at that time considered well of Jiang Qin.A
FromJiangbiao Zhuan: Sun Quan said to Jiang Qin, “Xu Sheng had challenged you in the past, and now you complement him. Are you trying to be like Qi Xi?” Qin replied, “I have heard that in recommending people for official affairs one does not consider old personal grudges. Xu Sheng is loyal, diligent, and strong, having great courage and strategies. He would make a superb commandant of ten thousand troops. Now, the great affairs of the world are not settled yet, and I should be helping the kingdom to promote talented people. Would I dare to conecal their talents just because I hold a grudge against them?” Sun Quan praised him for that.
When Sun Quan did battle with Guan Yu, Jinag Qin led the navy at Mian. On the way back, he fell ill and died. Sun Quan mourned for him personally in mourning apparel, and gave two hundred households and two hundred acres of land at Wuhu for the support of Jiang Qin’s family.
Jiang’s son, Yi, was made the Marquis of Xuancheng. He gained merits during the defence against Liu Bei, and returned to Nanjun where he fought with the Wei army. He died in battle there. Yi did not have offspring, and so his brother Xiu was given the military command, though he later was discharged due to misdemeanor.
